one_line_takeaway: A survey that systematizes machine unlearning into exact vs. approximate approaches and charts the open challenges for privacy-preserving, trustworthy, and adaptive ML.
core_contribution: A comprehensive survey and taxonomy of machine unlearning (the selective removal of specific training data points and their influence from an already-trained model). The paper's OWN contribution is organizational and analytical, not a new algorithm: (1) a structured two-branch taxonomy dividing solutions into "exact unlearning" (which fully removes data influence via algorithmic retraining) and "approximate unlearning" (which efficiently minimizes but does not fully eliminate residual influence); (2) a critical analysis of the surveyed methods' strengths, limitations, and open challenges; and (3) proposed future research directions. Within approximate unlearning it further sub-categorizes surveyed methods into influence-function-based, re-optimization-based, gradient-update-based, and graph-unlearning approaches (Tables II–V). Note: methods such as SISA, DaRE, and certified removal are works it CITES/surveys, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: This is a survey rather than an attack/defense paper, so the following is the threat context it SURVEYS, not a novel threat model it defines. The motivating adversaries are: (1) membership-inference and privacy-extraction adversaries who probe a trained model to determine whether specific (sensitive/personal) data was in the training set or to recover memorized data; and (2) data-poisoning adversaries who inject crafted malicious data to manipulate model behavior. Machine unlearning is framed as a mitigation — selectively removing offending data restores a model to behave as if that data were never used, reducing membership-inference success and removing poisoned points. The paper additionally cautions that unlearning itself can introduce a privacy risk (removing one point may leak information about neighboring points).
limitations: As a survey the paper presents no empirical evaluation, so its method comparisons are qualitative and not validated by measured performance; readers cannot compare methods numerically from it. Its scope centers on supervised learning and convex-model settings — it explicitly flags unlearning for non-convex models (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers) and for diverse/multimodal data (text, speech, video) as open, unaddressed problems. It also acknowledges that reliable verification of unlearning and the tension between efficiency, utility, and privacy remain unresolved in the surveyed literature.

datasets_used: None — this is a survey/taxonomy paper and reports no experiments of its own on any dataset.
quantitative_results: The paper reports NO original quantitative results; it has no experiments, benchmark tables, or numeric metrics of its own. Its comparisons (Tables I–V) are strictly qualitative, listing each surveyed method's Goal, Design Ideas, Strengths, and Weaknesses (e.g., efficiency, scalability, storage cost, accuracy degradation) rather than measured numbers.
For rigor of guarantees it cites (does not produce) the certified-removal notions ε-certified removal and (ε, δ)-certified removal from the literature it surveys.
baselines_compared: N/A — as a survey it compares no baselines against a proposed method. It instead categorizes and qualitatively compares surveyed methods, including SISA and DaRE (exact unlearning, Table I), influence-function-based methods (Table II), re-optimization-based methods including weight-scrubbing/certified-removal (Table III), gradient-update-based methods (Table IV), and approximate graph-unlearning methods (Table V).
